answer: Let \( \log_x(y^x) = 10 \) and \( \log_y(x^{4y}) = 10 \). Simplifying both equations gives \( y = x^{10} \) and \( x = y^{1/10} \), respectively. Substituting \( y = x^{10} \) into the second equation yields \( x = (x^{10})^{1/10} = x \), which holds true, so we solve \( y = x^{10} \) and \( x = y^{1/10} \) simultaneously, leading to \( xy = x \cdot x^{10} = x^{11} \).
